The car starts at position 0 with speed +1. For each 'A', it moves and doubles the speed. For 'R', it reverses direction, resetting speed to either -1 or +1 depending on the previous direction. The challenge is to find the minimum number of commands to reach the target.

Implementing in C++:

#include <deque>
#include <unordered_map>
#include <set>
#include <climits>

class Solution {
public:
    int racecar(int target) {
        std::deque<std::tuple<int, int, int>> q;
        q.push_back({0, 1, 0}); // pos, speed, steps

        // Visited map: position -> set of speeds
        std::unordered_map<int, std::unordered_set<int>> visited;
        visited[0].insert(1);

        while (!q.empty()) {
            int pos = std::get<0>(q.front());
            int speed = std::get<1>(q.front());
            int steps = std::get<2>(q.front());
            q.pop_front();

            if (pos == target) {
                return steps;
            }

            // Generate 'A' next state
            int new_pos_A = pos + speed;
            int new_speed_A = speed * 2;
            int new_steps = steps + 1;

            // Check bounds
            if (new_pos_A >= -target && new_pos_A <= 2 * target) {
                // Check if this (new_pos_A, new_speed_A) is visited
                if (visited.find(new_pos_A) == visited.end()) {
                    visited[new_pos_A] = std::unordered_set<int>{new_speed_A};
                    q.push_back({new_pos_A, new_speed_A, new_steps});
                } else if (visited[new_pos_A].find(new_speed_A) == visited[new_pos_A].end()) {
                    visited[new_pos_A].insert(new_speed_A);
                    q.push_back({new_pos_A, new_speed_A, new_steps});
                }
            }

            // Generate 'R' next state
            int new_speed_R = (speed > 0) ? -1 : 1;
            new_pos_A = pos; // same as pos

            if (new_pos_A >= -target && new_pos_A <= 2 * target) {
                if (visited.find(new_pos_A) == visited.end()) {
                    visited[new_pos_A] = std::unordered_set<int>{new_speed_R};
                    q.push_back({new_pos_A, new_speed_R, new_steps});
                } else if (visited[new_pos_A].find(new_speed_R) == visited[new_pos_A].end()) {
                    visited[new_pos_A].insert(new_speed_R);
                    q.push_back({new_pos_A, new_speed_R, new_steps});
                }
            }
        }

        return -1; // Should not reach here for valid target
    }
};

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int racecar(int target) {
        std::deque<std::tuple<int, int, int>> q;
        q.push_back({0, 1, 0}); // position, speed, steps

        std::unordered_map<int, std::unordered_set<int>> visited;
        visited[0].insert(1);

        while (!q.empty()) {
            int pos = std::get<0>(q.front());
            int speed = std::get<1>(q.front());
            int steps = std::get<2>(q.front());
            q.pop_front();

            if (pos == target) {
                return steps;
            }

            // Generate 'A' command
            long new_pos_A = static_cast<long>(pos) + speed;
            long new_speed_A = speed * 2;
            if (new_pos_A >= -static_cast<long>(target) && new_pos_A <= 2 * target) {
                if (visited.find(new_pos_A) == visited.end() || 
                    visited[new_pos_A].find(new_speed_A) == visited[new_pos_A].end()) {
                    if (visited.find(new_pos_A) == visited.end()) {
                        visited[new_pos_A] = {};
                    }
                    visited[new_pos_A].insert(new_speed_A);
                    q.push_back({static_cast<int>(new_pos_A), static_cast<int>(new_speed_A), steps + 1});
                }
            }

            // Generate 'R' command
            int new_speed_R = (speed > 0) ? -1 : 1;
            if (visited[pos].find(new_speed_R) == visited[pos].end()) {
                visited[pos].insert(new_speed_R);
                q.push_back({pos, new_speed_R, steps + 1});
            }
        }

        return -1;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(T log T)
space complexity: O(T log T)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses BFS to explore all possible car states (position, speed) while pruning states that exceed reasonable bounds (±target and 2*target). Visited tracking prevents redundant state exploration. Key strengths include efficient pruning and BFS's guarantee of finding the shortest path. Weaknesses include potential high memory usage for large targets.
